sentence: Please turn of the light.
correct: Please turn off the light.
explanation: The phrasal verb is <turn off> (two f's), not <turn of>.

sentence: I need to wake in early tomorrow.
correct: I need to wake up early tomorrow.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <wake up>, not <wake in>.

sentence: She picked on the phone.
correct: She picked up the phone.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <pick up> (to answer), not <pick on>.

sentence: Can you look out this word in the dictionary?
correct: Can you look up this word in the dictionary?
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <look up> (to search for information), not <look out>.

sentence: He put in his jacket because it was cold.
correct: He put on his jacket because it was cold.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <put on> (to wear), not <put in>.

sentence: Please sit up. The class is starting.
correct: Please sit down. The class is starting.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <sit down> (to take a seat), not <sit up>.

sentence: I get in at 7 am every day.
correct: I get up at 7 am every day.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <get up> (to rise from bed), not <get in>.

sentence: She takes of her shoes at the door.
correct: She takes off her shoes at the door.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <take off> (to remove), not <take of>.

sentence: Turn up the TV, please. It's too loud.
correct: Turn down the TV, please. It's too loud.
explanation: Use <turn down> to reduce volume. <Turn up> means to increase volume.

sentence: I look forward to see you soon.
correct: I look forward to seeing you soon.
explanation: After <look forward to>, use the -ing form: <to seeing>, not <to see>.

sentence: He gave in smoking last year.
correct: He gave up smoking last year.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <give up> (to stop doing), not <give in>.

sentence: Please come on. The door is open.
correct: Please come in. The door is open.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <come in> (to enter), not <come on>.

sentence: She goes in for a run every morning.
correct: She goes out for a run every morning.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <go out> (to leave the house), not <go in>.

sentence: I need to find up the answer.
correct: I need to find out the answer.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <find out> (to discover), not <find up>.

sentence: Can you help me clean on the kitchen?
correct: Can you help me clean up the kitchen?
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <clean up> (to tidy), not <clean on>.

sentence: He ran up of milk, so he went to the shop.
correct: He ran out of milk, so he went to the shop.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <run out of> (to have none left), not <run up of>.

sentence: She looks up to her older sister. She wants to be like her.
correct: She looks up to her older sister. She wants to be like her.
explanation: This sentence is correct. <Look up to> means to admire someone.

sentence: I need to throw out these old shoes.
correct: I need to throw out these old shoes.
explanation: This sentence is correct. <Throw out> means to discard or get rid of.

sentence: Please write up your name on the paper.
correct: Please write down your name on the paper.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <write down> (to note), not <write up>.

sentence: He turned off the TV and went to sleep.
correct: He turned off the TV and went to sleep.
explanation: This sentence is correct. <Turn off> means to switch off a device.

sentence: She set on the alarm for 6 am.
correct: She set up the alarm for 6 am.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <set up> (to prepare/configure), not <set on>.

sentence: I always mix on the ingredients before cooking.
correct: I always mix up the ingredients before cooking.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <mix up> (to combine), not <mix on>.

sentence: He hanged up the phone quickly.
correct: He hung up the phone quickly.
explanation: The past tense of <hang up> (phone) is <hung up>, not <hanged up>.

sentence: She filled up the form at the office.
correct: She filled in the form at the office.
She filled out the form at the office.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <fill in> or <fill out> (a form), not <fill up>.

sentence: I need to check on of the hotel by noon.
correct: I need to check out of the hotel by noon.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <check out of> (to leave a hotel), not <check on of>.
